quotes.verbatim_quote:
Although this is not a book about arpeggios, I feel it is important to include a few
     basic arpeggios so that you can see the picking method applied to arpeggios.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames the chapter as a demonstration that the alternate-picking system extends to arpeggios, not a comprehensive arpeggio study.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The difficulty with any arpeggio exercise , is keeping the notes from ringing. Articulatioin is more important
    han speed.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the central technical challenge of arpeggio picking: muting/separating notes, not playing fast.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Major triad exercises: This makes a great warm-up exercise. It is very difficult to
     play with clean articulation. The result is well worth the time. This exercie alone will
    facilitate the separtion of notes with any phrase that contains an arpeggio or arpeggio
     fragment.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Designates the major triad exercise as a foundational warm-up that develops note separation for all arpeggio playing.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Fingering and picking is the same throughout.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Signals that one fingering/picking pattern transposes across the arpeggio exercises, reinforcing the system's universality.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Minor 9 arpeggios: I have seen this referred to as sweep picking
      I see no reason to make any distinction. Everything follows the rule:
      Higher string = "down stroke" Lower string = "up stroke"
quotes.why_load_bearing: Bruno's core polemic: sweep picking is not a separate technique, just the directional rule applied to arpeggios.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Minor 11 arpeggios: Because of the fingering it is possible to play 6 notes in one beat.(sextuplet)
      The fingering sets up the consecutive down and up strokes to go across four strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Shows how fingering choice creates the consecutive same-direction strokes that enable sextuplet arpeggios across four strings.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Saxophone players
      and pianists use phrases like this.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Justifies arpeggio vocabulary by reference to non-guitar instruments, signaling these are general musical phrases, not guitar tricks.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Minor 9th arpeggios: Below are several ways to pick this. Again, the fingering makes
      the speed possible.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Restates the chapter thesis: fingering choice, not picking aggression, is what unlocks fast arpeggio playing.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I have found when there is a rest between phrases,
        it is possible to break the rule and use whatever stroke you
        please to start the phrase.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the one sanctioned exception to the higher-string/lower-string rule: phrase starts after rests can use either stroke.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Try starting any phrase with both strokes, down and up. There should be only a slieght difference in sound.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes a practice routine that verifies the picking method produces consistent tone regardless of starting stroke.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Minor 9th arpeggios:By using fingerings that place notes on adjacent strings, either higher or lower,
       these large two octave arpeggios become possible.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates the design principle for two-octave arpeggios: arrange notes on adjacent strings so the directional rule generates fluid picking.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I often run across guitarists
     who become misled into thinking that picking for single lines is different than picking for arpeggios.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the misconception the chapter exists to dispel: single-line and arpeggio picking are governed by one system.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You have to be careful
     to NOT let the fingerboard make the music. The music comes from inside; the picking mechanism described in this book
     is designed to make it easier for the music to be realized.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Closes the chapter with Bruno's aesthetic axiom: the picking system serves musical intent, never dictates it.
